title: Food and the Restaurant
description: Order a meal like a local: the three meals and the courses, polite ways to order (Para mí..., ¿Me trae...?, ¿Qué me recomienda?, La cuenta por favor), food and taste vocabulary, dietary needs (soy vegetariano/a, sin gluten), typical dishes (la paella, el gazpacho, las tapas), and the gustar/encantar structure.

cards.front: el desayuno, la comida / el almuerzo, la cena
cards.notes: The three meals: **el desayuno** (breakfast), **la comida** or **el almuerzo** (lunch — *la comida* is the usual word in Spain), and **la cena** (dinner). The verbs are *desayunar, comer/almorzar, cenar*.

cards.front: el primer plato, el segundo plato, el postre
cards.notes: A Spanish meal often comes in courses: **el primer plato** (starter/first course), **el segundo plato** (main course), and **el postre** (dessert). A fixed-price meal is **el menú del día**.

cards.front: Para mí..., ¿Me trae...?, La cuenta, por favor
cards.back: For me..., Could you bring me...?, The bill, please
cards.notes: Polite ordering phrases: **Para mí, la paella** (For me, the paella), **¿Me trae el menú?** (Could you bring me the menu?), and **La cuenta, por favor** (The bill, please). *Trae* is the polite *usted* form of *traer*.

cards.back: What do you recommend? / I would like... (lit. I wanted)
cards.notes: Ask the waiter **¿Qué me recomienda?** (What do you recommend?, *usted* form). To order softly, Spanish uses the imperfect: **Yo quería el gazpacho** (literally 'I wanted', but it means 'I'd like') — sounds more polite than *quiero*.

cards.front: los mariscos, la ternera, el cordero, las gambas
cards.notes: Common menu words: **los mariscos** (shellfish/seafood), **la ternera** (beef or veal), **el cordero** (lamb), and **las gambas** (prawns/shrimp). Note: in Latin America prawns are often **los camarones**.

cards.front: las verduras, los champiñones, el ajo
cards.notes: **las verduras** (vegetables), **los champiñones** (mushrooms), **el ajo** (garlic). *El ajo* is everywhere in Spanish cooking — *gambas al ajillo* (garlic prawns) is a classic tapa.

cards.front: picante, soso, salado, dulce, amargo, rico
cards.back: spicy, bland, salty, sweet, bitter, tasty
cards.notes: Describing taste: **picante** (spicy/hot), **soso** (bland, under-salted), **salado** (salty), **dulce** (sweet), **amargo** (bitter), **rico** (delicious/tasty). Use *estar* for taste: *Está muy rico* = It's really tasty.

cards.front: Está rico (tastes good) ≠ Es rico (he is rich)
cards.back: estar rico = tastes good; ser rico = is wealthy
cards.notes: Watch the verb! **Está rico** = it *tastes* delicious (temporary, about the food). **Es rico** = he/she/it *is* rich (wealthy). So *El pastel está rico* = the cake is yummy, but *Mi tío es rico* = my uncle is wealthy.

cards.front: Me gustan las gambas. / Me encanta la paella.
cards.back: I like prawns. / I love the paella.
cards.notes: **gustar** works backwards from English: the thing liked is the subject. *Me gustan las gambas* (lit. 'prawns please me') — plural thing → **gustan**; singular → **gusta**. Never *Yo gusto*. **Encantar** ('to love') works the same: *Me encanta la paella*.

cards.front: Soy vegetariano/a. Soy alérgico/a a... Sin gluten.
cards.back: I'm vegetarian. I'm allergic to... Gluten-free.
cards.notes: Dietary needs: **Soy vegetariano/a** (I'm vegetarian), **Soy alérgico/a a los mariscos** (I'm allergic to shellfish), **sin gluten** (gluten-free), **sin lactosa** (lactose-free). Match the *-o/-a* ending to yourself.

cards.front: estar embarazada = to be pregnant (NOT embarrassed!)
cards.notes: False friend alert! **embarazada** does **not** mean 'embarrassed' — it means **pregnant**. To say you're embarrassed, use **avergonzado/a** or **tener vergüenza**: *Estoy avergonzada* / *Me da vergüenza*. So *Estoy embarazada* announces a baby, not awkwardness!

cards.front: la paella, el gazpacho, las tapas, la tortilla española
cards.back: paella, cold tomato soup, small plates, Spanish omelette
cards.notes: Typical dishes: **la paella** (rice with seafood/meat), **el gazpacho** (cold tomato-and-vegetable soup), **las tapas** (small shared plates), and **la tortilla española** (a thick potato-and-egg omelette — not a Mexican tortilla!).

steps.title: Meals, courses and the menu
steps.body:
# Eating out in Spanish

First, the three meals of the day:

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| el desayuno | breakfast |
| la comida / el almuerzo | lunch |
| la cena | dinner |

In Spain, **la comida** is the normal word for the midday meal; *el almuerzo* is common in Latin America.

In a restaurant, a full meal comes in **courses**:

- **el primer plato** — first course / starter
- **el segundo plato** — main course
- **el postre** — dessert

A great-value option is **el menú del día** — a fixed-price lunch with several courses and a drink.

> Heads-up: **la tortilla española** is a thick *potato-and-egg omelette*, not a flat Mexican tortilla.

steps.title: Ordering politely
steps.body:
## How to order without sounding rude

Spanish has neat fixed phrases for the table. Many use the polite **usted** verb form:

- **¿Me trae el menú, por favor?** — Could you bring me the menu? (*trae* = polite form of *traer*)
- **¿Qué me recomienda?** — What do you recommend?
- **Para mí, la paella.** — For me, the paella. (great for going round the table)
- **Yo quería el gazpacho.** — I'd like the gazpacho. (literally 'I wanted' — the imperfect sounds softer and more polite than *quiero*)
- **La cuenta, por favor.** — The bill, please.

> The little word **me** in *¿Me trae...?* and *¿Qué me recomienda?* is an indirect object — 'to/for me'. It makes the request personal and polite.

steps.title: Liking food, and a famous false friend
steps.body:
## gustar, encantar and how things taste

**gustar** literally means 'to be pleasing', so it works **backwards** from English. The thing you like is the subject:

- **Me gusta la paella.** — I like paella. (singular thing → *gusta*)
- **Me gustan las gambas.** — I like prawns. (plural thing → *gustan*)
- **Me encanta el gazpacho.** — I love the gazpacho.

> ⚠️ Never say *Yo gusto las gambas*. That would mean 'I am pleasing'! Always **Me gustan las gambas**.

**Describing taste** uses *estar*: **Está muy rico** (it's delicious), **Está soso** (it's bland), **Está salado** (too salty), **picante** (spicy), **dulce** (sweet), **amargo** (bitter).

> ⚠️ **estar rico** vs **ser rico**: *La sopa está rica* = the soup tastes great. *Mi tío es rico* = my uncle is wealthy.

**False friend!** **embarazada** means **pregnant**, NOT 'embarrassed'. If you ate too much and feel awkward, say **Estoy avergonzado/a** or **Me da vergüenza** — not *Estoy embarazada* (which announces a baby)!

**Dietary needs:** *Soy vegetariano/a*, *Soy alérgico/a a los mariscos*, *sin gluten*.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish word with its English meaning.
Translate politely: *Could you bring me the menu, please?*
accept: me trae el menu por favor
Me trae el menú, por favor
exercise.hint: Use ¿Me trae...? (polite usted form of traer). menu = el menú / la carta.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I like prawns.* (careful with the gustar structure)
accept: A mí me gustan las gambas.
exercise.hint: The thing liked is the subject. Prawns are plural → Me gustan las gambas. Never 'Yo gusto'.

exercise.prompt: You spilled your wine and feel awkward. Which sentence correctly says 'I'm embarrassed'? (Watch the false friend!)
exercise.hint: embarazada/embarazado relates to pregnancy, not embarrassment. 'embarrassed' = avergonzado/a (with estar).

exercise.prompt: Fill in the verb. The soup tastes delicious (talking about the food, not wealth).
exercise.hint: For how food tastes, use estar, not ser. Está rica = it tastes great; es rica would mean 'is wealthy'.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to ask the waiter: *What do you recommend (to me)?*
exercise.hint: ¿Qué me recomienda? — 'me' is the indirect object (to me); recomienda is the usted form.

exercise.prompt: A woman says: *I'm vegetarian and I'm allergic to shellfish.*
accept: Soy vegetariana y soy alérgica a los mariscos.
soy vegetariana y soy alergica a los mariscos
Soy vegetariana y alérgica a los mariscos.
Soy vegetariana y soy alérgica a los mariscos
exercise.hint: Use ser: Soy vegetariana, soy alérgica a... A woman uses the -a endings.
distractors: Estoy vegetariana y estoy alérgica a los mariscos.
Soy vegetariano y soy alérgico a los mariscos.
Soy embarazada y soy alérgica a los mariscos.

exercise.prompt: Fill the gap to order: 'For me, the lamb.'
exercise.hint: 'For me' = Para mí. This is the easy way to order around the table.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish taste word with its English meaning.

exercise.hint: the bill = la cuenta. Add 'por favor' to be polite.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the verb. 'I love the paella' (encantar works like gustar).
exercise.hint: La paella is singular, so the verb is singular: Me encanta la paella.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to order politely: *I'd like the gazpacho.* (use the soft 'quería')
exercise.hint: Spanish softens an order with the imperfect: Yo quería... = 'I'd like...' (literally 'I wanted').
